```markdown
## AI-Augmented Development

This project was built using [Claude Code](https://claude.com/code) as an AI pair programming partner.

### How AI Was Used
- **Architecture & Planning:** Initial system design, technology selection, and trade-off analysis
- **Implementation:** Component scaffolding, form logic, API endpoints, database migrations
- **Testing:** Test strategy, test case generation, edge case identification
- **Code Review:** Iterative refinement of implementation details
- **Documentation:** README structure, inline documentation

### AI Session Transcripts
- [Planning Session](link-to-gist-or-file) - Architecture decisions and tech stack selection
- [Implementation Session](link-to-gist-or-file) - Core booking flow development
- [Testing & Refinement Session](link-to-gist-or-file) - Test writing and bug fixes

### AI Configuration
- See `CLAUDE.md` for project-level AI context
- See `.claude/` for custom commands and planning artifacts
- See `docs/decisions/` for AI-assisted Architecture Decision Records
```

This directly addresses the assessment requirement: "We want to see how you architect the solution, guide the AI, and troubleshoot issues."

---

## 6. Other Repo-Level Signals

**a) `.claude/settings.json` (committed, team-shared)**
- Shows intentional permission configuration
- Example: allowing specific tools, setting environment variables, enabling extended thinking

**b) `.github/copilot-instructions.md`**
- Even if not using Copilot, having this shows awareness of multi-tool AI configuration
- Can mirror key parts of CLAUDE.md

**c) CI/CD with AI-aware steps (advanced, probably skip for time)**
- GitHub Actions that run AI-generated tests
- Lint rules that AI was configured to follow

**d) `.editorconfig` / `prettier.config.js` / `eslint.config.js`**
- These indirectly support AI development by ensuring Claude's output matches project formatting
- When Claude sees these in the project, it respects them

**Key source:**
- [Trail of Bits claude-code-config](https://github.com/trailofbits/claude-code-config)
- [feiskyer/claude-code-settings](https://github.com/feiskyer/claude-code-settings)

## 7. Claude Code Specific Features

**a) Memory files (auto-memory)**
- Claude Code automatically writes notes based on corrections and preferences
- Toggle via `/memory` command
- Stored in `~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/`

**b) Custom slash commands (`.claude/commands/`)**
- Create markdown files that become `/project:<name>` commands
- Examples for this assessment:
  - `plan.md` - "Analyze the booking flow requirements and create an implementation plan"
  - `review.md` - "Review the current code against the assessment requirements checklist"
  - `test.md` - "Generate tests for the most recently changed files"

**c) Skills (`.claude/skills/`)**
- More powerful than commands: Claude can autonomously invoke them
- SKILL.md with YAML frontmatter (name, description, allowed-tools)
- Can reference subagents for isolated, focused work

**d) Plan mode (Shift+Tab twice)**
- Creates read-only planning documents
- Set `plansDirectory` in settings.json to save plans to a committed directory
- Plans survive context compaction and can be reviewed in PRs
- Excellent for the initial architecture session

**e) Hooks**
- Deterministic scripts at lifecycle events: PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Notification, Stop
- Example: PostToolUse hook that runs Prettier after every file edit
- Example: Notification hook that sends desktop notification when Claude finishes
- Configured in `.claude/settings.json` under the `hooks` key

- For this assessment, a GitHub MCP server could be useful for PR management
- Configured in `.claude/settings.json`

input.content:
# CLAUDE.md - OTA Booking Engine Assessment

## Project Overview

Building a multi-step OTA booking form for a LekkeSlaap interview assessment. The form handles date selection, room/guest allocation, booking summary, submission, and status display. Authentication is required to submit but not to browse.

## Tech Stack

- **Backend:** Laravel + Breeze + Inertia.js (PHP)
- **Frontend:** React + TypeScript + Tailwind CSS
- **Components:** shadcn/ui (Radix primitives)
- **Forms:** React Hook Form + Zod (zodResolver)
- **State:** Zustand with persist middleware (localStorage)
- **Database:** SQLite (development)
- **Testing:** Pest (PHP backend), Vitest + React Testing Library (frontend)
- **Auth:** Laravel Breeze (session-based)

## Key Commands

```bash
php artisan serve                          # Start Laravel dev server
npm run dev                                # Start Vite dev server
php artisan test                           # Run Pest tests
npx vitest                                 # Run frontend tests
php artisan queue:work --stop-when-empty   # Process queued jobs
php artisan migrate                        # Run database migrations
php artisan migrate:fresh --seed           # Reset and seed database
```

## Architecture Notes

- **Inertia.js** bridges Laravel controllers to React pages (no separate API layer)
- **API routes** used alongside Inertia for async operations (booking status polling)
- **Server-authoritative pricing** - client calculates for display, server validates on submission
- **ChannelManagerService** is slow (1-4s) and unreliable (25% failure) - use Laravel queue jobs with retry
- **Polling** (every 2-3s) for booking status updates on the frontend
- **Property data** cached server-side (rarely changes) with Cache-Control headers
- **Zustand persist** stores wizard state in localStorage (survives refresh and auth redirect)
- **15% VAT** shown in tooltip over total price

## Code Style

### General
- Descriptive variable and function names
- Comments only where they add value - for hard-to-understand code or to explain non-obvious decisions
- Parse don't validate (Zod for parsing, not just checking)
- No code duplication - extract reusable logic
- Performance-conscious (code splitting, lazy loading where appropriate)
- Fail fast - validate early, return early
- No magic numbers - use Tailwind config, constants, or named variables
- Watch for global side effects when editing shared UI components

### TypeScript
- Strict mode enabled
- Prefer type inference where types are obvious
- Use Zod schemas as the source of truth for types (z.infer<typeof schema>)
- No `any` types unless absolutely necessary with a comment explaining why

### Tailwind CSS
- Use config files for design tokens (colors, spacing, etc.)
- No magic numbers in class strings
- Always use cn() for class composition when more than one Tailwind class is used
- Brand colors: orange (#EC772D), dark gray (#5A5B5D), white (#FFFFFF)

### React
- Functional components only
- Custom hooks for reusable logic
- Keep components focused - one responsibility per component
- Use loading spinners for async operations

### PHP/Laravel
- Follow Laravel conventions for controllers, models, services
- Form Request classes for validation
- Resource classes for API responses where needed

## Testing Rules
- Test all logic, but don't overtest
- Never test third-party library behavior - assume it works, test our integration
- Each test must add clear value
- Backend: Pest for validation, price integrity, job dispatch, API endpoints
- Frontend: Vitest + RTL for form logic, price calculations, state management
- Prefer testing behavior over implementation details

## Git & Workflow
- Branch naming: sc-{ticket}/feature/{description}
- Conventional Commits: feat:, fix:, test:, docs:, refactor:, chore:
- Enforced by commitlint + Husky
- Annotated tags only (pre-push hook rejects lightweight tags)
- PRs target main
- Always ask before committing - never auto-commit
- Small, atomic commits - one logical change per commit

## Writing Style
- Never use em dashes in any text output - use regular dashes, commas, or restructure sentences
- Be concise and direct
- Use plain language

## Key Constraints
- 3-5 hour time budget - prioritize MVP, document what you'd add with more time
- User is untrustworthy - validate everything server-side
- ChannelManagerService is unreliable - queue jobs with retry, never block the user
- Form state must survive page refresh and auth redirect
- Fully accessible (WCAG 2.1 AA) - keyboard navigation, screen reader support, semantic HTML
- Property data from property.json is the source of truth for rooms and pricing

input.content:
# Open Questions - Awaiting Byron's Input

These are unresolved decisions and ambiguities from the README and task requirements. Each needs a decision before (or during) implementation. Where I have a recommendation, I've noted it.

---

## Architecture

### Q1. Are you comfortable using Inertia.js?

You said you've never used it. After reading my explanation, are you on board with using Breeze + Inertia (React), or would you prefer a decoupled approach (Laravel API + standalone React SPA)?

**My recommendation:** Inertia. It saves significant time (no API layer to build), auth works out of the box, and LekkeSlaap's actual site appears to use it. The learning curve is manageable since you still write normal React components - data just arrives as props from controllers instead of API calls.

**If you say no:** We go decoupled - Laravel Sanctum for API auth, React SPA with Vite, TanStack Query for data fetching. More familiar to you but more boilerplate.

---

## Booking Logic

### Q2. Can a user book multiple room types in one booking?

Example: 1 Deluxe Suite + 2 Standard Rooms in a single booking.

The `available_count` field on each unit suggests multiple units can be booked. But should they be combinable?

**Options:**
- **A) Yes, multi-room bookings** - More complex UI (room selection grid), more complex pricing, but realistic for a real OTA
- **B) No, single room type per booking** - Simpler form, still demonstrates all the required skills

**My recommendation:** Option A (multi-room). It's what a real OTA does, and it makes the room selection step more interesting. The pricing logic is slightly more complex but not dramatically so.

---

### Q3. How should guest allocation work?

When a user selects rooms, how do they assign guests?

**Options:**
- **A) Per-room-type guest count** - User selects "2x Standard Room, 3 guests total." Simpler UI.
- **B) Per-room guest assignment** - User selects "Standard Room 1: 2 guests, Standard Room 2: 1 guest." More granular but more complex.
- **C) Total guest count only** - User enters total guests, we validate it fits within selected rooms' combined max_guests.

**My recommendation:** Option A. Per-room-type guest count. It's the pattern most OTAs use (Booking.com does this). For `per_person` pricing, we need a guest count anyway. For `per_unit` pricing, we just validate guests don't exceed max_guests.

---

### Q4. Pricing model confirmation

Based on the data, my interpretation:

| Unit | Model | Calculation |
|------|-------|-------------|
| Standard Room (per_unit, R1,200) | R1,200 x nights x quantity | Guest count doesn't affect price |
| Deluxe Suite (per_unit, R2,500) | R2,500 x nights x quantity | Guest count doesn't affect price |
| Family Cottage (per_person, R650) | R650 x guests x nights x quantity | Guest count drives price |
| Backpacker Bunk (per_person, R250) | R250 x guests x nights x quantity | Guest count drives price (max 1 guest, so effectively per_unit) |

Then 15% VAT on the total.

Is this correct? Or does `per_person` mean something different?

---

### Q5. What data does ChannelManagerService::book() expect?

The task says "pass the final validated data" but doesn't define the schema. I'd send something like:

```php
[
    'property_id' => 101,
    'check_in' => '2026-04-01',
    'check_out' => '2026-04-03',
    'rooms' => [
        ['unit_id' => 1, 'quantity' => 2, 'guests' => 4],
        ['unit_id' => 2, 'quantity' => 1, 'guests' => 3],
    ],
    'total_price' => 15400.00,
    'tax_amount' => 2310.00,
    'currency' => 'ZAR',
    'guest_name' => 'Byron Devin',
    'guest_email' => 'byron@example.com',
]
```

Does this look right, or would you add/remove fields?

---

## Date Handling

### Q6. Date constraints

- **Minimum stay:** 1 night? (check-out must be at least 1 day after check-in)
- **Maximum future booking:** Unlimited, or cap at e.g. 1 year?
- **Same-day check-in:** Allow or block?
- **Past dates:** Obviously blocked, but starting from today or tomorrow?

**My recommendation:** Min 1 night, no max cap (keep it simple), allow same-day check-in, block past dates (starting from today). These are reasonable assumptions to document in the README.

---

## User Data

### Q7. What fields do we collect at submission?

Auth gives us name and email. Do we also collect:
- Phone number?
- Special requests / notes?
- Number of adults vs children?

**My recommendation:** Just use auth user data (name, email). Keep it minimal. The task focuses on the booking flow, not a full checkout form. Add a "special requests" textarea if time permits - it's one field and shows attention to UX.

---

## Currency & Formatting

### Q8. ZAR formatting convention

South African Rand has two common formats:
- **International:** R1,200.00 (comma thousands, dot decimal)
- **South African convention:** R1 200,00 (space thousands, comma decimal)

LekkeSlaap's website uses: `R 1 500` (space after R, space thousands, no decimals for whole numbers).

**My recommendation:** Match LekkeSlaap's format: `R 1 200` for whole numbers, `R 1 200.00` when showing cents. Use `Intl.NumberFormat('en-ZA', { style: 'currency', currency: 'ZAR' })` which outputs `R 1 200,00`.

---

## UX Edge Cases

### Q9. What happens to persisted state after a successful booking?

After a booking is confirmed, should we:
- **A) Clear all wizard state** from localStorage immediately
- **B) Keep it until the user starts a new booking**
- **C) Clear it and redirect to a "booking confirmed" page**

**My recommendation:** Option C. Clear state on successful submission, show a dedicated confirmation page with the booking details and confirmation ID.

---

### Q10. Booking history - show a "My Bookings" list?

The task says "Display the booking status." Does this mean:
- **A) Just the status page for the booking they just made** (minimal)
- **B) A "My Bookings" page listing all past bookings** (more work)

**My recommendation:** Option A for MVP. The task says "Display the booking status" (singular). A full bookings list is a "future enhancement" for the README.

---

### Q11. UI design approach

The task says design doesn't matter. But should we:
- **A) Pure shadcn defaults** - zero design time
- **B) shadcn defaults + LekkeSlaap brand colors** (orange #EC772D, white, dark gray #5A5B5D) in Tailwind config - 10 minutes of effort
- **C) More polished** - brand colors + property images from the JSON + layout inspired by their site

**My recommendation:** Option B. Tiny time investment, shows brand awareness.

---

### Q12. Polling for booking status - confirmed?

After submitting a booking, the frontend needs to know when the queued job finishes. I recommended polling (GET /api/bookings/{id}/status every 2-3 seconds). This is simpler than WebSockets/SSE and appropriate for the time constraint.

Are you good with polling?

---

### Q13. AI showcase - which level?

From the options I gave you, which do you want?

**Minimum (recommended for time):**
- CLAUDE.md (done)
- Session transcripts via /export
- Co-Authored-By on commits (automatic)
- README section on AI workflow
- key-decisions.md (done)

**Extended:**
- All the above, plus:
- .claude/commands/ with custom slash commands
- Plan mode outputs saved to docs/plans/
- .claude/settings.json committed

**Full (time-permitting):**
- All the above, plus:
- ADR files in docs/decisions/
- Feature branches with PR descriptions
- Hooks configuration

---

## Summary of Questions

| # | Question | Quick answer needed |
|---|----------|-------------------|
| Q1 | Inertia.js - yes or no? | Yes / No |
| Q2 | Multi-room bookings? | Yes / No |
| Q3 | Guest allocation model? | A (per-type) / B (per-room) / C (total only) |
| Q4 | Pricing model correct? | Yes / No / Correction |
| Q5 | ChannelManager data schema OK? | Yes / No / Changes |
| Q6 | Date constraints OK? | Yes / No / Changes |
| Q7 | Collect extra fields or just auth data? | Minimal / Add phone / Add requests |
| Q8 | ZAR format? | R 1 200 / R1,200.00 / Other |
| Q9 | Clear state after booking? | A / B / C |
| Q10 | Booking history page? | Just status / Full list |
| Q11 | UI design approach? | A / B / C |
| Q12 | Polling confirmed? | Yes / No |
| Q13 | AI showcase level? | Minimum / Extended / Full |

1→# Key Decisions - OTA Booking Engine
     2→
     3→## Status Key
     4→- DECIDED - Decision made, rationale documented
     5→- PENDING - Awaiting Byron's input
     6→- RECOMMENDATION - Analysis done, leaning toward a choice
     7→
     8→---
     9→
    10→## 1. Frontend Framework
    11→
    12→**Decision:** React with TypeScript
    13→
    14→**Status:** DECIDED
    15→
    16→**Explanation:** The task allows React or Vue. Byron's core stack is React/TypeScript, and LekkeSlaap uses React in production.
    17→
    18→**Options:**
    19→| Option | Pros | Cons |
    20→|--------|------|------|
    21→| React + TypeScript | Byron's strongest stack, matches LekkeSlaap's production stack, huge ecosystem | None for this context |
    22→| Vue + TypeScript | Also allowed by task | Not Byron's primary framework, less strategic alignment |
    23→
    24→**Decision rationale:** React is Byron's strength and aligns with what LekkeSlaap actually uses. TypeScript adds type safety and demonstrates production maturity.
    25→
    26→---
    27→
    28→## 2. Application Architecture - Laravel Breeze + Inertia.js vs Decoupled API
    29→
    30→**Decision:** PENDING
    31→
    32→**Status:** PENDING
    33→
    34→**Explanation:** The task recommends Laravel Breeze for auth scaffolding. Breeze with React ships with Inertia.js (server-driven SPA). The alternative is a decoupled approach: Laravel API backend + standalone React SPA.
    35→
    36→**Options:**
    37→| Option | Pros | Cons |
    38→|--------|------|------|
    39→| Laravel Breeze + Inertia.js (React) | Recommended by task, handles auth boilerplate, SSR-like data passing, fast setup, shows Laravel competency | Inertia is opinionated, less common outside Laravel ecosystem, Byron hasn't used it before |
    40→| Decoupled: Laravel API + React SPA (Vite) | Clean separation, more familiar pattern for Byron, standard REST/API approach | More auth boilerplate to wire up, task explicitly recommends Breeze |
    41→| Laravel Breeze + Inertia.js, but use API routes where needed | Best of both - Breeze for auth scaffolding, Inertia for page routing, API routes for async operations (booking status polling) | Slight complexity of mixing patterns |
    42→
    43→**Recommendation:** Option 3 (Breeze + Inertia hybrid). It follows the task's recommendation, saves time on auth, and shows willingness to work within the Laravel ecosystem (directly relevant since LekkeSlaap uses Laravel). We can still use API routes for things like booking submission and status polling.
    44→
    45→---
    46→
    47→## 3. Authentication Approach
    48→
    49→**Decision:** PENDING
    50→
    51→**Status:** PENDING
    52→
    53→**Explanation:** Auth is needed but is not the focus. Users browse as guests, must auth to submit. State must persist through login/signup.
    54→
    55→**Options:**
    56→| Option | Pros | Cons |
    57→|--------|------|------|
    58→| Laravel Breeze (built-in) | Task explicitly recommends it, zero extra dependencies, session-based auth works perfectly with Inertia, handles CSRF automatically | Basic UI (but design isn't being tested) |
    59→| Clerk | Polished auth UI, social logins, easy setup | External dependency, overkill for assessment scope, task recommends Breeze, adds complexity to state persistence across auth, requires API keys/account setup |
    60→| Laravel Fortify + custom UI | More control over auth UI | More work than Breeze for same result |
    61→
    62→**Recommendation:** Laravel Breeze. The task literally tells you to use it. Using Clerk would mean ignoring their recommendation, adding external dependencies, and spending time on something they said to shortcut. Breeze's session-based auth also makes "preserve form state on login" trivial since the session persists.
    63→
    64→---
    65→
    66→## 4. Database
    67→
    68→**Decision:** PENDING
    69→
    70→**Status:** PENDING
    71→
    72→**Explanation:** Need to persist bookings. Task says SQLite is preconfigured with Breeze.
    73→
    74→**Options:**
    75→| Option | Pros | Cons |
    76→|--------|------|------|
    77→| SQLite | Zero config, preconfigured with Breeze, task mentions it, good enough for assessment | Not production-grade for high-traffic |
    78→| MySQL | Matches LekkeSlaap's production stack, demonstrates awareness | Extra setup steps, time spent on non-core logic |
    79→| PostgreSQL | Byron's most familiar DB | Not in LekkeSlaap's stack, extra setup |
    80→
    81→**Recommendation:** SQLite for speed. The task says "keep in mind that the core booking logic should be your main focus." Mention in the README that MySQL/MariaDB would be used in production to match their stack.
    82→
    83→---
    84→
    85→## 5. UI Component Library
    86→
    87→**Decision:** PENDING
    88→
    89→**Status:** PENDING
    90→
    91→**Explanation:** Need date picker, tooltips, form components. Task says "use any third-party libraries, don't build complex components from scratch."
    92→
    93→**Options:**
    94→| Option | Pros | Cons |
    95→|--------|------|------|
    96→| shadcn/ui + Radix primitives | Beautiful defaults, accessible, Tailwind-native, copy-paste (not dependency), highly customizable, great with cn() | Setup takes a few minutes |
    97→| Headless UI + custom styling | Fully accessible, minimal | More styling work |
    98→| Material UI / Ant Design | Lots of components | Heavy, not Tailwind-native, opinionated styling |
    99→| Mantine | Good DX, built-in hooks | Not Tailwind-native |
   100→
   101→**Recommendation:** shadcn/ui. It's Tailwind-native, accessible by default (built on Radix), works perfectly with cn(), and the components are copied into your project (not a dependency). Matches Byron's Tailwind preference perfectly.
   102→
   103→---
   104→
   105→## 6. UI Design Approach
   106→
   107→**Decision:** PENDING
   108→
   109→**Status:** PENDING
   110→
   111→**Explanation:** The task states "you are not being tested on your design, you can use default components and styling." Time is 3-5 hours.
   112→
   113→**Options:**
   114→| Option | Pros | Cons |
   115→|--------|------|------|
   116→| Match LekkeSlaap's look and feel | Shows initiative and cultural alignment, impressive | Significant time investment for something they said doesn't matter |
   117→| Clean minimal (shadcn defaults) | Fast, professional-looking by default, time spent on logic instead | Doesn't show design initiative |
   118→| Minimal with LekkeSlaap color palette/branding touches | Small time investment, shows awareness without going overboard | Minor effort |
   119→
   120→**Recommendation:** Option 3. Use shadcn defaults but add LekkeSlaap's brand colors (teal/green palette) to the Tailwind config and maybe use their logo placeholder. 10 minutes of effort that shows you thought about their brand without wasting time on design.
   121→
   122→---
   123→
   124→## 7. Form Handling
   125→
   126→**Decision:** React Hook Form + Zod (via zodResolver)
   127→
   128→**Status:** DECIDED
   129→
   130→**Explanation:** Multi-step form with validation across steps, server-side validation mirroring.
   131→
   132→**Options:**
   133→| Option | Pros | Cons |
   134→|--------|------|------|
   135→| React Hook Form + Zod | Industry standard, excellent multi-step support, Zod schemas can be shared/reused, zodResolver integrates cleanly, uncontrolled by default (performant) | None significant |
   136→| Formik + Yup | Older but proven | Heavier, controlled inputs by default, less performant |
   137→| Native React state | No dependencies | Massive boilerplate for multi-step validation |
   138→
   139→**Decision rationale:** React Hook Form + Zod is the right tool for this. Zod schemas define validation once and can be reused for both client and conceptual parity with server-side validation. The "parse don't validate" principle aligns perfectly with Zod's parse-based approach.
   140→
   141→---
   142→
   143→## 8. Multi-Step Form State Management
   144→
   145→**Decision:** PENDING
   146→
   147→**Status:** PENDING
   148→
   149→**Explanation:** Need to manage state across 5 steps, persist on refresh, preserve through auth flow.
   150→
   151→**Options:**
   152→| Option | Pros | Cons |
   153→|--------|------|------|
   154→| Zustand + persist middleware | Simple, lightweight, built-in persistence to localStorage/sessionStorage, works outside React tree | Extra dependency |
   155→| React Context + useReducer + manual localStorage | No extra dependency | More boilerplate, context re-renders |
   156→| React Hook Form context (FormProvider) + localStorage sync | Form state and UI state unified | Tighter coupling, harder to persist complex state |
   157→| Inertia shared data + server session | Server-authoritative, survives refresh natively | Less reactive, round-trip for state changes |
   158→
   159→**Recommendation:** Zustand with persist middleware. It's tiny (~1KB), the persist middleware handles localStorage automatically, and it works cleanly alongside React Hook Form (Zustand for wizard state/selections, RHF for form field validation). This makes "survive refresh" and "survive auth redirect" trivial.
   160→
   161→---
   162→
   163→## 9. State Persistence Strategy (Survive Refresh)
   164→
   165→**Decision:** PENDING
   166→
   167→**Status:** PENDING
   168→
   169→**Explanation:** Task requires: "Users must be able to go back and forth between steps without losing progress (even on refresh)."
   170→
   171→**Options:**
   172→| Option | Pros | Cons |
   173→|--------|------|------|
   174→| localStorage (via Zustand persist) | Survives refresh, survives auth redirect, client-side, fast | Cleared if user clears browser data |
   175→| sessionStorage | Scoped to tab, survives refresh | Doesn't survive new tab |
   176→| Server-side session (Laravel) | Most robust, survives device switch | Round-trip latency, more backend work |
   177→| URL params | Shareable, bookmarkable | Complex state doesn't serialize well, exposes data |
   178→
   179→**Recommendation:** localStorage via Zustand persist as primary. This is the simplest approach that meets all requirements: survives refresh, survives auth redirect (critical requirement), and is fast. For a production app, you'd add server-side session backup.
   180→
   181→---
   182→
   183→## 10. Handling the Unreliable ChannelManagerService
   184→
   185→**Decision:** PENDING
   186→
   187→**Status:** PENDING
   188→
   189→**Explanation:** The service is slow (1-4s) and fails 25% of the time. Task says: "user is not left staring at a frozen browser" and "a temporary network hiccup does not permanently lose a booking."
   190→
   191→**Architecture:**
   192→1. User submits booking -> Laravel saves to DB with status "pending"
   193→2. Laravel dispatches a queued job to call ChannelManagerService
   194→3. Job retries on failure (Laravel's built-in retry)
   195→4. Frontend polls for status updates (or uses SSE/WebSockets)
   196→
   197→**Frontend status update options:**
   198→| Option | Pros | Cons |
   199→|--------|------|------|
   200→| Polling (every 2-3s) | Simple, works everywhere, easy to implement | Slightly wasteful requests |
   201→| Server-Sent Events (SSE) | Real-time, one-way (server to client), simple | Needs specific Laravel setup |
   202→| WebSockets (Laravel Echo + Pusher/Soketi) | True real-time, bidirectional | Overkill, significant setup time |
   203→
   204→**Recommendation:** Polling. Simple, reliable, appropriate for 3-5 hour scope. The booking confirmation typically resolves in 1-4 seconds, so a few poll requests is negligible. Show a nice status UI with progress indication.
   205→
   206→**Retry strategy:** Laravel's built-in job retry with exponential backoff. 3 attempts, then mark as "failed" and show user a "we're still working on it" message with option to check back.
   207→
   208→---
   209→
   210→## 11. Property Data Caching
   211→
   212→**Decision:** PENDING
   213→
   214→**Status:** PENDING
   215→
   216→**Explanation:** Task says property data "rarely changes hour-to-hour. Optimise how your backend delivers this to the frontend."
   217→
   218→**Options:**
   219→| Option | Pros | Cons |
   220→|--------|------|------|
   221→| Laravel Cache (file/database driver) with TTL | Simple, no extra services, shows caching awareness | File cache is single-server |
   222→| Redis cache | Production-grade, fast, matches LekkeSlaap stack | Requires Redis running locally |
   223→| HTTP Cache-Control headers + ETag | Browser caching, reduces requests entirely | Less control over invalidation |
   224→| Inertia shared data (cached on server) | Automatic with Inertia, clean | Coupled to Inertia |
   225→
   226→**Recommendation:** Laravel's built-in cache with file driver (for assessment simplicity) + Cache-Control headers on the API response. Cache for 1 hour. Mention in README that Redis would be used in production. This demonstrates understanding of caching strategy without over-engineering.
   227→
   228→---
   229→
   230→## 12. Price Calculation - Server Authority
   231→
   232→**Decision:** Server-side is source of truth, client calculates for UX
   233→
   234→**Status:** DECIDED
   235→
   236→**Explanation:** Task says "assume user is untrustworthy" and "no manipulated prices pass backend validation."
   237→
   238→**Approach:**
   239→- Client calculates prices for real-time display (responsive UX)
   240→- Server recalculates and validates all prices on submission (source of truth)
   241→- If client and server prices don't match, reject the submission
   242→- Price logic is defined once (Zod schema for shape, server for calculation authority)
   243→
   244→---
   245→
   246→## 13. Testing Strategy
   247→
   248→**Decision:** PENDING
   249→
   250→**Status:** PENDING
   251→
   252→**Explanation:** Need meaningful tests that prove our logic works. Don't overtest. Don't test third-party libraries.
   253→
   254→**Options:**
   255→| Layer | Tool | What to test |
   256→|-------|------|-------------|
   257→| Backend unit/feature | Pest (Laravel) | Booking validation, price calculation, job dispatch, API endpoints |
   258→| Frontend unit | Vitest + React Testing Library | Form step logic, price calculation display, state management |
   259→| E2E | Playwright | Full booking flow happy path |
   260→| API integration | Pest | ChannelManagerService retry logic, booking status transitions |
   261→
   262→**Key question:** Given 3-5 hours, which layers are highest priority?
   263→
   264→**Recommendation:** Focus on:
   265→1. Backend feature tests (Pest) - booking validation and price integrity (this is the "system integrity" requirement)
   266→2. Frontend unit tests (Vitest) - price calculation, form validation logic
   267→3. One E2E test if time permits - happy path booking flow
   268→
   269→Skip: auth tests (Breeze handles this), component rendering tests (testing the library), trivial getter tests.
   270→
   271→---
   272→
   273→## 14. Error Handling and Monitoring
   274→
   275→**Decision:** PENDING
   276→
   277→**Status:** PENDING
   278→
   279→**Explanation:** How to handle and report errors.
   280→
   281→**Options:**
   282→| Option | Pros | Cons |
   283→|--------|------|------|
   284→| Laravel's built-in logging + structured error responses | Sufficient for assessment, shows clean error handling | No external observability |
   285→| Sentry integration | Impressive, production-grade, shows ops awareness | Extra setup time, external dependency, account needed |
   286→| Laravel logging + mention Sentry in README | Shows awareness without burning time | Doesn't demonstrate implementation |
   287→
   288→**Recommendation:** Option 3. Use Laravel's built-in logging with structured error handling. Mention Sentry in the README as a "production enhancement." Time is better spent on core booking logic. Clean error handling in the code itself (try/catch, user-friendly messages, proper HTTP status codes) matters more than monitoring setup for an assessment.
   289→
   290→---
   291→
   292→## 15. Loading States
   293→
   294→**Decision:** PENDING
   295→
   296→**Status:** PENDING
   297→
   298→**Explanation:** UX during data fetching and transitions.
   299→
   300→**Options:**
   301→| Option | Pros | Cons |
   302→|--------|------|------|
   303→| Loading skeletons | Modern, polished UX, prevents layout shift | Minor implementation time |
   304→| Spinner | Simple, universal | Dated, layout shift |
   305→| Skeleton for initial load, spinner for actions | Best of both | Slightly more code |
   306→
   307→**Recommendation:** Skeletons for the room/property data loading (content-heavy), spinner/loading state for form submission. shadcn/ui has a Skeleton component built-in, so this is nearly free.
   308→
   309→---
   310→
   311→## 16. AI-Augmented Engineering Showcase
   312→
   313→**Decision:** PENDING
   314→
   315→**Status:** PENDING
   316→
   317→**Explanation:** Task explicitly encourages AI tools and requires chat logs. How to maximize this demonstration.
   318→
   319→**Ideas:**
   320→- CLAUDE.md file at repo root with project context, conventions, and AI instructions
   321→- .claude/ folder with planning docs and session artifacts
   322→- Key decisions doc (this file) showing AI-assisted architectural thinking
   323→- Git history showing AI-assisted commits (Co-Authored-By)
   324→- README section on AI workflow
   325→- This planning conversation itself as a transcript
   326→
   327→**Recommendation:** Include CLAUDE.md, this key-decisions doc, and reference the AI session in the README. The git history with Co-Authored-By tags on commits naturally demonstrates the workflow. Don't over-engineer the showcase - the quality of the code and decisions IS the showcase.
   328→
   329→---
   330→
   331→## 17. Accessibility (a11y)
   332→
   333→**Decision:** Follow WCAG 2.1 AA baseline
   334→
   335→**Status:** DECIDED
   336→
   337→**Explanation:** Task says "fully usable by everyone, regardless of how they navigate the web." This means keyboard navigation, screen reader support, and semantic HTML.
   338→
   339→**Approach:**
   340→- shadcn/ui (Radix primitives) provides accessible components out of the box
   341→- Semantic HTML (form, fieldset, legend, label, button)
   342→- ARIA attributes where needed (step indicator, live regions for price updates)
   343→- Keyboard navigation through all steps
   344→- Focus management on step transitions
   345→- Color contrast (shadcn defaults meet AA)
   346→
   347→---
   348→
   349→## 18. Project Structure
   350→
   351→**Decision:** PENDING
   352→
   353→**Status:** PENDING
   354→
   355→**Explanation:** How to organize the Laravel + React codebase.
   356→
   357→**Recommendation:** Standard Laravel Breeze structure:
   358→```
   359→/
   360→  app/
   361→    Http/Controllers/
   362→    Models/
   363→    Services/
   364→      ChannelManagerService.php
   365→    Jobs/
   366→  resources/
   367→    js/
   368→      Components/
   369→        Booking/
   370→          DateStep.tsx
   371→          RoomStep.tsx
   372→          SummaryStep.tsx
   373→          StatusStep.tsx
   374→          BookingWizard.tsx
   375→      Pages/
   376→        Booking/
   377→          Index.tsx
   378→      Hooks/
   379→      Lib/
   380→        schemas/       (Zod schemas)
   381→        utils/
   382→      Stores/          (Zustand)
   383→    css/
   384→  routes/
   385→  database/
   386→    migrations/
   387→  tests/
   388→    Feature/
   389→    Unit/
   390→```
   391→
   392→---
   393→
   394→## Decisions Still Pending
   395→
   396→1. Architecture - Breeze + Inertia vs decoupled API (Decision 2)
   397→2. Auth - Breeze vs Clerk (Decision 3)
   398→3. Database - SQLite vs MySQL (Decision 4)
   399→4. Component library - shadcn/ui confirmation (Decision 5)
   400→5. UI design approach (Decision 6)
   401→6. State management - Zustand confirmation (Decision 8)
   402→7. State persistence approach (Decision 9)
   403→8. Background job frontend pattern (Decision 10)
   404→9. Caching strategy (Decision 11)
   405→10. Testing depth and priority (Decision 13)
   406→11. Error monitoring - Sentry or not (Decision 14)
   407→12. Loading states (Decision 15)
   408→13. AI showcase approach (Decision 16)
   409→
   410→---
   411→
   412→## Future Enhancements (Document for README)
   413→
   414→Things to mention as "what I'd add with more time":
   415→- Redis caching in production
   416→- Sentry error monitoring
   417→- WebSocket-based real-time booking status (Laravel Echo)
   418→- Rate limiting on booking endpoints

# Key Decisions - OTA Booking Engine

## Status Key
- DECIDED - Decision made, rationale documented
- PENDING - Awaiting Byron's input
- RECOMMENDATION - Analysis done, leaning toward a choice

---

## 1. Frontend Framework

**Decision:** React with TypeScript

**Status:** DECIDED

**Explanation:** The task allows React or Vue. Byron's core stack is React/TypeScript, and LekkeSlaap uses React in production.

**Options:**
| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| React + TypeScript | Byron's strongest stack, matches LekkeSlaap's production stack, huge ecosystem | None for this context |
| Vue + TypeScript | Also allowed by task | Not Byron's primary framework, less strategic alignment |

**Decision rationale:** React is Byron's strength and aligns with what LekkeSlaap actually uses. TypeScript adds type safety and demonstrates production maturity.

---

## 2. Application Architecture - Laravel Breeze + Inertia.js vs Decoupled API

**Decision:** PENDING

**Status:** PENDING

**Explanation:** The task recommends Laravel Breeze for auth scaffolding. Breeze with React ships with Inertia.js (server-driven SPA). The alternative is a decoupled approach: Laravel API backend + standalone React SPA.

**Options:**
| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| Laravel Breeze + Inertia.js (React) | Recommended by task, handles auth boilerplate, SSR-like data passing, fast setup, shows Laravel competency | Inertia is opinionated, less common outside Laravel ecosystem, Byron hasn't used it before |
| Decoupled: Laravel API + React SPA (Vite) | Clean separation, more familiar pattern for Byron, standard REST/API approach | More auth boilerplate to wire up, task explicitly recommends Breeze |
| Laravel Breeze + Inertia.js, but use API routes where needed | Best of both - Breeze for auth scaffolding, Inertia for page routing, API routes for async operations (booking status polling) | Slight complexity of mixing patterns |

**Recommendation:** Option 3 (Breeze + Inertia hybrid). It follows the task's recommendation, saves time on auth, and shows willingness to work within the Laravel ecosystem (directly relevant since LekkeSlaap uses Laravel). We can still use API routes for things like booking submission and status polling.

---

## 3. Authentication Approach

**Decision:** PENDING

**Status:** PENDING

**Explanation:** Auth is needed but is not the focus. Users browse as guests, must auth to submit. State must persist through login/signup.

**Options:**
| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| Laravel Breeze (built-in) | Task explicitly recommends it, zero extra dependencies, session-based auth works perfectly with Inertia, handles CSRF automatically | Basic UI (but design isn't being tested) |
| Clerk | Polished auth UI, social logins, easy setup | External dependency, overkill for assessment scope, task recommends Breeze, adds complexity to state persistence across auth, requires API keys/account setup |
| Laravel Fortify + custom UI | More control over auth UI | More work than Breeze for same result |

**Recommendation:** Laravel Breeze. The task literally tells you to use it. Using Clerk would mean ignoring their recommendation, adding external dependencies, and spending time on something they said to shortcut. Breeze's session-based auth also makes "preserve form state on login" trivial since the session persists.

---

## 4. Database

**Decision:** PENDING

**Status:** PENDING

**Explanation:** Need to persist bookings. Task says SQLite is preconfigured with Breeze.

**Options:**
| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| SQLite | Zero config, preconfigured with Breeze, task mentions it, good enough for assessment | Not production-grade for high-traffic |
| MySQL | Matches LekkeSlaap's production stack, demonstrates awareness | Extra setup steps, time spent on non-core logic |
| PostgreSQL | Byron's most familiar DB | Not in LekkeSlaap's stack, extra setup |

**Recommendation:** SQLite for speed. The task says "keep in mind that the core booking logic should be your main focus." Mention in the README that MySQL/MariaDB would be used in production to match their stack.

---

## 5. UI Component Library

**Decision:** PENDING

**Status:** PENDING

**Explanation:** Need date picker, tooltips, form components. Task says "use any third-party libraries, don't build complex components from scratch."

**Options:**
| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| shadcn/ui + Radix primitives | Beautiful defaults, accessible, Tailwind-native, copy-paste (not dependency), highly customizable, great with cn() | Setup takes a few minutes |
| Headless UI + custom styling | Fully accessible, minimal | More styling work |
| Material UI / Ant Design | Lots of components | Heavy, not Tailwind-native, opinionated styling |
| Mantine | Good DX, built-in hooks | Not Tailwind-native |

**Recommendation:** shadcn/ui. It's Tailwind-native, accessible by default (built on Radix), works perfectly with cn(), and the components are copied into your project (not a dependency). Matches Byron's Tailwind preference perfectly.

---

## 6. UI Design Approach

**Decision:** PENDING

**Status:** PENDING

**Explanation:** The task states "you are not being tested on your design, you can use default components and styling." Time is 3-5 hours.

**Options:**
| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| Match LekkeSlaap's look and feel | Shows initiative and cultural alignment, impressive | Significant time investment for something they said doesn't matter |
| Clean minimal (shadcn defaults) | Fast, professional-looking by default, time spent on logic instead | Doesn't show design initiative |
| Minimal with LekkeSlaap color palette/branding touches | Small time investment, shows awareness without going overboard | Minor effort |

**Recommendation:** Option 3. Use shadcn defaults but add LekkeSlaap's brand colors (teal/green palette) to the Tailwind config and maybe use their logo placeholder. 10 minutes of effort that shows you thought about their brand without wasting time on design.

---

## 7. Form Handling

**Decision:** React Hook Form + Zod (via zodResolver)

**Status:** DECIDED

**Explanation:** Multi-step form with validation across steps, server-side validation mirroring.

**Options:**
| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| React Hook Form + Zod | Industry standard, excellent multi-step support, Zod schemas can be shared/reused, zodResolver integrates cleanly, uncontrolled by default (performant) | None significant |
| Formik + Yup | Older but proven | Heavier, controlled inputs by default, less performant |
| Native React state | No dependencies | Massive boilerplate for multi-step validation |

**Decision rationale:** React Hook Form + Zod is the right tool for this. Zod schemas define validation once and can be reused for both client and conceptual parity with server-side validation. The "parse don't validate" principle aligns perfectly with Zod's parse-based approach.

---

## 8. Multi-Step Form State Management

**Decision:** PENDING

**Status:** PENDING

**Explanation:** Need to manage state across 5 steps, persist on refresh, preserve through auth flow.

**Options:**
| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| Zustand + persist middleware | Simple, lightweight, built-in persistence to localStorage/sessionStorage, works outside React tree | Extra dependency |
| React Context + useReducer + manual localStorage | No extra dependency | More boilerplate, context re-renders |
| React Hook Form context (FormProvider) + localStorage sync | Form state and UI state unified | Tighter coupling, harder to persist complex state |
| Inertia shared data + server session | Server-authoritative, survives refresh natively | Less reactive, round-trip for state changes |

**Recommendation:** Zustand with persist middleware. It's tiny (~1KB), the persist middleware handles localStorage automatically, and it works cleanly alongside React Hook Form (Zustand for wizard state/selections, RHF for form field validation). This makes "survive refresh" and "survive auth redirect" trivial.

---

## 9. State Persistence Strategy (Survive Refresh)

**Decision:** PENDING

**Status:** PENDING

**Explanation:** Task requires: "Users must be able to go back and forth between steps without losing progress (even on refresh)."

**Options:**
| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| localStorage (via Zustand persist) | Survives refresh, survives auth redirect, client-side, fast | Cleared if user clears browser data |
| sessionStorage | Scoped to tab, survives refresh | Doesn't survive new tab |
| Server-side session (Laravel) | Most robust, survives device switch | Round-trip latency, more backend work |
| URL params | Shareable, bookmarkable | Complex state doesn't serialize well, exposes data |

**Recommendation:** localStorage via Zustand persist as primary. This is the simplest approach that meets all requirements: survives refresh, survives auth redirect (critical requirement), and is fast. For a production app, you'd add server-side session backup.

---

## 10. Handling the Unreliable ChannelManagerService

**Decision:** PENDING

**Status:** PENDING

**Explanation:** The service is slow (1-4s) and fails 25% of the time. Task says: "user is not left staring at a frozen browser" and "a temporary network hiccup does not permanently lose a booking."

**Architecture:**
1. User submits booking -> Laravel saves to DB with status "pending"
2. Laravel dispatches a queued job to call ChannelManagerService
3. Job retries on failure (Laravel's built-in retry)
4. Frontend polls for status updates (or uses SSE/WebSockets)

**Frontend status update options:**
| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| Polling (every 2-3s) | Simple, works everywhere, easy to implement | Slightly wasteful requests |
| Server-Sent Events (SSE) | Real-time, one-way (server to client), simple | Needs specific Laravel setup |
| WebSockets (Laravel Echo + Pusher/Soketi) | True real-time, bidirectional | Overkill, significant setup time |

**Recommendation:** Polling. Simple, reliable, appropriate for 3-5 hour scope. The booking confirmation typically resolves in 1-4 seconds, so a few poll requests is negligible. Show a nice status UI with progress indication.

**Retry strategy:** Laravel's built-in job retry with exponential backoff. 3 attempts, then mark as "failed" and show user a "we're still working on it" message with option to check back.

---

## 11. Property Data Caching

**Decision:** PENDING

**Status:** PENDING

**Explanation:** Task says property data "rarely changes hour-to-hour. Optimise how your backend delivers this to the frontend."

**Options:**
| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| Laravel Cache (file/database driver) with TTL | Simple, no extra services, shows caching awareness | File cache is single-server |
| Redis cache | Production-grade, fast, matches LekkeSlaap stack | Requires Redis running locally |
| HTTP Cache-Control headers + ETag | Browser caching, reduces requests entirely | Less control over invalidation |
| Inertia shared data (cached on server) | Automatic with Inertia, clean | Coupled to Inertia |

**Recommendation:** Laravel's built-in cache with file driver (for assessment simplicity) + Cache-Control headers on the API response. Cache for 1 hour. Mention in README that Redis would be used in production. This demonstrates understanding of caching strategy without over-engineering.

---

## 12. Price Calculation - Server Authority

**Decision:** Server-side is source of truth, client calculates for UX

**Status:** DECIDED

**Explanation:** Task says "assume user is untrustworthy" and "no manipulated prices pass backend validation."

**Approach:**
- Client calculates prices for real-time display (responsive UX)
- Server recalculates and validates all prices on submission (source of truth)
- If client and server prices don't match, reject the submission
- Price logic is defined once (Zod schema for shape, server for calculation authority)

---

## 13. Testing Strategy

**Decision:** PENDING

**Status:** PENDING

**Explanation:** Need meaningful tests that prove our logic works. Don't overtest. Don't test third-party libraries.

**Options:**
| Layer | Tool | What to test |
|-------|------|-------------|
| Backend unit/feature | Pest (Laravel) | Booking validation, price calculation, job dispatch, API endpoints |
| Frontend unit | Vitest + React Testing Library | Form step logic, price calculation display, state management |
| E2E | Playwright | Full booking flow happy path |
| API integration | Pest | ChannelManagerService retry logic, booking status transitions |

**Key question:** Given 3-5 hours, which layers are highest priority?

**Recommendation:** Focus on:
1. Backend feature tests (Pest) - booking validation and price integrity (this is the "system integrity" requirement)
2. Frontend unit tests (Vitest) - price calculation, form validation logic
3. One E2E test if time permits - happy path booking flow

Skip: auth tests (Breeze handles this), component rendering tests (testing the library), trivial getter tests.

---

## 14. Error Handling and Monitoring

**Decision:** PENDING

**Status:** PENDING

**Explanation:** How to handle and report errors.

**Options:**
| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| Laravel's built-in logging + structured error responses | Sufficient for assessment, shows clean error handling | No external observability |
| Sentry integration | Impressive, production-grade, shows ops awareness | Extra setup time, external dependency, account needed |
| Laravel logging + mention Sentry in README | Shows awareness without burning time | Doesn't demonstrate implementation |

**Recommendation:** Option 3. Use Laravel's built-in logging with structured error handling. Mention Sentry in the README as a "production enhancement." Time is better spent on core booking logic. Clean error handling in the code itself (try/catch, user-friendly messages, proper HTTP status codes) matters more than monitoring setup for an assessment.

---

## 15. Loading States

**Decision:** PENDING

**Status:** PENDING

**Explanation:** UX during data fetching and transitions.

**Options:**
| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| Loading skeletons | Modern, polished UX, prevents layout shift | Minor implementation time |
| Spinner | Simple, universal | Dated, layout shift |
| Skeleton for initial load, spinner for actions | Best of both | Slightly more code |

**Recommendation:** Skeletons for the room/property data loading (content-heavy), spinner/loading state for form submission. shadcn/ui has a Skeleton component built-in, so this is nearly free.

---

## 16. AI-Augmented Engineering Showcase

**Decision:** PENDING

**Status:** PENDING

**Explanation:** Task explicitly encourages AI tools and requires chat logs. How to maximize this demonstration.

**Ideas:**
- CLAUDE.md file at repo root with project context, conventions, and AI instructions
- .claude/ folder with planning docs and session artifacts
- Key decisions doc (this file) showing AI-assisted architectural thinking
- Git history showing AI-assisted commits (Co-Authored-By)

**Recommendation:** Include CLAUDE.md, this key-decisions doc, and reference the AI session in the README. The git history with Co-Authored-By tags on commits naturally demonstrates the workflow. Don't over-engineer the showcase - the quality of the code and decisions IS the showcase.

**Status:** DECIDED

**Explanation:** Task says "fully usable by everyone, regardless of how they navigate the web." This means keyboard navigation, screen reader support, and semantic HTML.

**Approach:**
- shadcn/ui (Radix primitives) provides accessible components out of the box

- ARIA attributes where needed (step indicator, live regions for price updates)
- Keyboard navigation through all steps
- Focus management on step transitions
- Color contrast (shadcn defaults meet AA)

---

## 18. Project Structure

**Decision:** PENDING

**Status:** PENDING

**Explanation:** How to organize the Laravel + React codebase.

**Recommendation:** Standard Laravel Breeze structure:
```
/
  app/

    Models/
    Services/
      ChannelManagerService.php
    Jobs/
  resources/

      Components/
        Booking/
          DateStep.tsx

  routes/
  database/
    migrations/

```

---

## Decisions Still Pending

2. Auth - Breeze vs Clerk (Decision 3)
3. Database - SQLite vs MySQL (Decision 4)

input.content:
# Key Decisions - OTA Booking Engine

## Status Key
- DECIDED - Decision made, rationale documented
- PENDING - Awaiting Byron's input (see open-questions.md)

---

## 1. Frontend Framework

**Decision:** React with TypeScript

**Status:** DECIDED

**Explanation:** The task allows React or Vue. Byron's core stack is React/TypeScript, and LekkeSlaap uses React in production.

**Options:**
| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| React + TypeScript | Byron's strongest stack, matches LekkeSlaap's production stack, huge ecosystem | None for this context |
| Vue + TypeScript | Also allowed by task | Not Byron's primary framework, less strategic alignment |

**Decision rationale:** React is Byron's strength and aligns with what LekkeSlaap actually uses. TypeScript adds type safety and demonstrates production maturity.

---

## 2. Application Architecture - Laravel Breeze + Inertia.js vs Decoupled API

**Decision:** PENDING (see open-questions.md Q1)

**Status:** PENDING

**Explanation:** The task recommends Laravel Breeze for auth scaffolding. Breeze with React ships with Inertia.js (server-driven SPA). The alternative is a decoupled approach: Laravel API backend + standalone React SPA. Byron has not used Inertia before.

**Options:**
| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| Laravel Breeze + Inertia.js (React) | Recommended by task, handles auth boilerplate, SSR-like data passing, fast setup, shows Laravel competency, LekkeSlaap's own site appears to use Inertia (Ziggy routes detected) | Inertia is opinionated, less common outside Laravel ecosystem, Byron hasn't used it before |
| Decoupled: Laravel API + React SPA (Vite) | Clean separation, more familiar pattern for Byron, standard REST/API approach | More auth boilerplate to wire up, task explicitly recommends Breeze |
| Laravel Breeze + Inertia.js, but use API routes where needed | Best of both - Breeze for auth scaffolding, Inertia for page routing, API routes for async operations (booking status polling) | Slight complexity of mixing patterns |

**Recommendation:** Option 3 (Breeze + Inertia hybrid). It follows the task's recommendation, saves time on auth, and shows willingness to work within the Laravel ecosystem. We can still use API routes for things like booking submission and status polling. LekkeSlaap's actual site uses this pattern (Ziggy + React detected in their source).

---

## 3. Authentication Approach

**Decision:** Laravel Breeze (built-in)

**Status:** DECIDED

**Explanation:** Auth is needed but is not the focus. Users browse as guests, must auth to submit. State must persist through login/signup.

**Options:**
| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| **Laravel Breeze (built-in)** | Task explicitly recommends it, zero extra dependencies, session-based auth works perfectly with Inertia, handles CSRF automatically | Basic UI (but design isn't being tested) |
| Clerk | Polished auth UI, social logins, easy setup | External dependency, overkill for assessment scope, task recommends Breeze, adds complexity to state persistence across auth, requires API keys/account setup |
| Laravel Fortify + custom UI | More control over auth UI | More work than Breeze for same result |

**Decision rationale:** The task literally says "We recommend using a starter kit like Laravel Breeze." Breeze's session-based auth makes "preserve form state on login" trivial since the session persists. Wizard state in localStorage (via Zustand) also survives the auth redirect naturally. Clerk was considered but rejected as overkill and contrary to the task's recommendation.

---

## 4. Database

**Decision:** SQLite

**Status:** DECIDED

**Explanation:** Need to persist bookings. Task says SQLite is preconfigured with Breeze and that "the core booking logic should be your main focus."

**Options:**
| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| **SQLite** | Zero config, preconfigured with Breeze, task mentions it, good enough for assessment | Not production-grade for high-traffic |
| MySQL | Matches LekkeSlaap's production stack (MySQL/MariaDB), demonstrates awareness | Extra setup steps, time spent on non-core logic |
| PostgreSQL | Byron's most familiar DB | Not in LekkeSlaap's stack, extra setup |

**Decision rationale:** SQLite saves time for zero downside in an assessment context. README will note that MySQL/MariaDB would be used in production to match LekkeSlaap's infrastructure.

---

## 5. UI Component Library

**Decision:** shadcn/ui + Tailwind CSS

**Status:** DECIDED

**Explanation:** Need date picker, tooltips, form components. Task says "use any third-party libraries, don't build complex components from scratch." The task does not specify a component library. LekkeSlaap's own site uses custom CSS with Pikaday for date picking and imgix React SDK for images.

**Options:**
| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| **shadcn/ui + Radix primitives** | Beautiful defaults, accessible, Tailwind-native, copy-paste (not dependency), highly customizable, great with cn() | Setup takes a few minutes |
| Headless UI + custom styling | Fully accessible, minimal | More styling work |
| Material UI / Ant Design | Lots of components | Heavy, not Tailwind-native, opinionated styling |
| Mantine | Good DX, built-in hooks | Not Tailwind-native |

**Decision rationale:** shadcn/ui is Tailwind-native, accessible by default (built on Radix), works perfectly with cn(), and components are copied into the project (not a dependency). Has all needed components: DatePicker, Tooltip, Card, Button, Form, Dialog. Matches Byron's Tailwind-first approach.

---

## 6. UI Design Approach

**Decision:** PENDING (see open-questions.md Q11)

**Status:** PENDING

**Explanation:** The task states "you are not being tested on your design, you can use default components and styling." Time is 3-5 hours.

**Options:**
| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| Pure shadcn defaults | Fast, professional-looking by default, time spent on logic instead | Doesn't show brand awareness |
| shadcn defaults + LekkeSlaap brand colors | Small time investment (~10 min), shows awareness without going overboard | Minor effort |
| More polished with property images and layout inspiration | Shows initiative | Significant time for something they said doesn't matter |

**Brand colors (confirmed from LekkeSlaap's site):**
| Color | Hex | Usage |
|-------|-----|-------|
| Orange | `#EC772D` | Primary brand accent, CTAs, badges |
| Dark gray | `#5A5B5D` | Text/secondary |
| White | `#FFFFFF` | Backgrounds |

**Recommendation:** Option 2. shadcn defaults with LekkeSlaap's orange (#EC772D) as the primary color in Tailwind config. Minimal time, shows brand awareness.

---

## 7. Form Handling

**Decision:** React Hook Form + Zod (via zodResolver)

**Status:** DECIDED

**Explanation:** Multi-step form with validation across steps, server-side validation mirroring.

**Options:**
| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| **React Hook Form + Zod** | Industry standard, excellent multi-step support, Zod schemas can be shared/reused, zodResolver integrates cleanly, uncontrolled by default (performant) | None significant |
| Formik + Yup | Older but proven | Heavier, controlled inputs by default, less performant |
| Native React state | No dependencies | Massive boilerplate for multi-step validation |

**Decision rationale:** React Hook Form + Zod is the right tool for this. Zod schemas define validation once and can be reused for both client and conceptual parity with server-side validation. The "parse don't validate" principle aligns perfectly with Zod's parse-based approach.

---

## 8. Multi-Step Form State Management

**Decision:** Zustand with persist middleware

**Status:** DECIDED

**Explanation:** Need to manage state across 5 steps, persist on refresh, preserve through auth flow.

**Options:**
| Option | Size | Boilerplate | Persistence | Provider needed | Learning curve |
|--------|------|-------------|-------------|----------------|---------------|
| **Zustand + persist** | ~1KB | Minimal | Built-in middleware | No | Low |
| Redux Toolkit | ~11KB | Moderate (slices, reducers) | Needs redux-persist | Yes (Provider) | Medium |
| React Context + useReducer | 0KB (built-in) | High (reducer, context, provider, manual localStorage sync) | Manual | Yes | Low but verbose |
| Jotai | ~2KB | Minimal (atomic) | Plugin | No | Low |

**Decision rationale:** Zustand is tiny (~1KB), requires no Provider wrapper, and its persist middleware handles localStorage automatically. Works cleanly alongside React Hook Form (Zustand for wizard state/selections, RHF for form field validation). The persist middleware makes "survive refresh" and "survive auth redirect" a one-line config instead of manual wiring.

---

## 9. State Persistence Strategy (Survive Refresh)

**Decision:** localStorage via Zustand persist

**Status:** DECIDED

**Explanation:** Task requires: "Users must be able to go back and forth between steps without losing progress (even on refresh)."

**Options:**
| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| **localStorage (via Zustand persist)** | Survives refresh, survives auth redirect, client-side, fast | Cleared if user clears browser data |
| sessionStorage | Scoped to tab, survives refresh | Doesn't survive new tab |
| Server-side session (Laravel) | Most robust, survives device switch | Round-trip latency, more backend work |
| URL params | Shareable, bookmarkable | Complex state doesn't serialize well, exposes data |

**Decision rationale:** localStorage via Zustand persist is the simplest approach that meets all requirements: survives refresh, survives auth redirect (critical), and is fast. README will note that production would add server-side session backup for cross-device support.

---

## 10. Handling the Unreliable ChannelManagerService

**Decision:** Queue job with retry + frontend polling

**Status:** DECIDED (pending confirmation on polling - see open-questions.md Q12)

**Explanation:** The service is slow (1-4s) and fails 25% of the time. Task says: "user is not left staring at a frozen browser" and "a temporary network hiccup does not permanently lose a booking."

**Architecture:**
1. User submits booking -> Laravel saves to DB with status "pending"
2. Laravel dispatches a queued job to call ChannelManagerService
3. Job retries on failure (Laravel's built-in retry with exponential backoff, 3 attempts)
4. Frontend polls for status updates every 2-3 seconds
5. User sees a status page immediately (not a frozen browser)

**Frontend status update options:**
| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| **Polling (every 2-3s)** | Simple, works everywhere, easy to implement | Slightly wasteful requests |
| Server-Sent Events (SSE) | Real-time, one-way | Needs specific Laravel setup |
| WebSockets (Laravel Echo + Pusher/Soketi) | True real-time, bidirectional | Overkill, significant setup time |

**Decision rationale:** Polling is appropriate because the booking resolves in 1-4 seconds (3-6 poll requests max). No additional infrastructure needed. Task hints at this approach: "process pending jobs manually by running php artisan queue:work." WebSockets/SSE noted as production enhancement in README.

---

## 11. Property Data Caching

**Decision:** Laravel file cache + Cache-Control headers

**Status:** DECIDED

**Explanation:** Task says property data "rarely changes hour-to-hour. Optimise how your backend delivers this to the frontend."

**Options:**
| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| **Laravel Cache (file driver) with TTL** | Simple, no extra services, shows caching awareness | File cache is single-server |
| Redis cache | Production-grade, fast, matches LekkeSlaap stack | Requires Redis running locally |
| HTTP Cache-Control headers + ETag | Browser caching, reduces requests entirely | Less control over invalidation |

**Decision rationale:** Laravel's built-in cache with file driver for assessment simplicity, plus Cache-Control headers. Cache for 1 hour. README notes Redis would be used in production. This demonstrates caching strategy without over-engineering.

---

## 12. Price Calculation - Server Authority

**Decision:** Server-side is source of truth, client calculates for UX

**Status:** DECIDED

**Explanation:** Task says "assume user is untrustworthy" and "no manipulated prices pass backend validation."

**Approach:**
- Client calculates prices for real-time display (responsive UX)
- Server recalculates and validates all prices on submission (source of truth)
- If client and server prices don't match, reject the submission
- Price logic is defined once (Zod schema for shape, server for calculation authority)

---

## 13. Testing Strategy

**Decision:** Pest (backend) + Vitest (frontend)

**Status:** DECIDED

**Explanation:** Need meaningful tests that prove our logic works. Don't overtest. Don't test third-party libraries.

| Layer | Tool | What to test |
|-------|------|-------------|
| Backend unit/feature | Pest | Booking validation, price calculation, job dispatch, API endpoints |
| Frontend unit | Vitest + React Testing Library | Form step logic, price calculation display, state management |
| E2E (if time permits) | Playwright | Full booking flow happy path |

**Priority order (given 3-5 hour constraint):**
1. Backend feature tests (Pest) - booking validation and price integrity (the "system integrity" requirement)
2. Frontend unit tests (Vitest) - price calculation, form validation logic
3. One E2E test if time permits - happy path booking flow

**Skip:** Auth tests (Breeze handles this), component rendering tests (testing the library), trivial getter tests.

**Decision rationale:** Pest chosen for PHP backend because it has cleaner syntax (similar to Jest/Vitest) and is the modern Laravel testing standard. Vitest for frontend as Byron's preferred tool. Both layers need testing because the task emphasizes backend validation ("no manipulated prices") and frontend UX.

---

## 14. Error Handling and Monitoring

**Decision:** Structured error handling, no Sentry

**Status:** DECIDED

**Explanation:** Focus on clean error handling in code, mention Sentry as production enhancement.

**Approach:**
- Structured JSON error responses from the API
- Proper HTTP status codes (422 for validation, 500 for server errors, 503 for ChannelManager failures)
- User-friendly error messages on the frontend (no raw error dumps)
- Laravel's built-in logging for server-side errors
- Try/catch with meaningful error boundaries in React
- Graceful degradation when ChannelManagerService fails

**Decision rationale:** Sentry skipped - extra setup time for something that doesn't demonstrate core engineering skills. Clean error handling in the code itself (structured responses, proper HTTP codes, user-friendly messages) matters more for an assessment. Sentry noted as production enhancement in README.

---

## 15. Loading States

**Decision:** Spinners

**Status:** DECIDED

**Explanation:** UX during data fetching and transitions.

**Options:**
| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| Loading skeletons | Modern, polished UX, prevents layout shift | Minor implementation time |
| **Spinner** | Simple, universal, fast to implement | Dated, layout shift |
| Skeleton for initial load, spinner for actions | Best of both | Slightly more code |

**Decision rationale:** Spinners for all loading states. Simple, fast to implement, appropriate for time constraint. Skeletons noted as enhancement for polish.

---

## 16. AI-Augmented Engineering Showcase

**Decision:** PENDING (see open-questions.md Q13)

**Status:** PENDING

**Explanation:** Task explicitly encourages AI tools and requires chat logs. Need to decide scope of demonstration.

**Must-haves (near-zero time cost):**
- CLAUDE.md at repo root (done)
- Session transcripts via /export (assessment requirement)
- Co-Authored-By tags on commits (automatic with Claude Code)
- README section on AI workflow with links to transcripts
- key-decisions.md (this file, done)

**High-value (small time investment):**
- Conventional Commits with clear messages (already in workflow)
- .claude/commands/ with 1-2 custom slash commands
- Plan mode outputs saved to docs/plans/

**Medium-value (if time permits):**
- .claude/settings.json committed
- Feature branches with PR descriptions
- Individual ADR files in docs/decisions/

**Advanced (impressive but likely skip for time):**
- Hooks configuration
- MCP servers
- Skills definitions

---

## 17. Accessibility (a11y)

**Decision:** Follow WCAG 2.1 AA baseline

**Status:** DECIDED

**Explanation:** Task says "fully usable by everyone, regardless of how they navigate the web." This means keyboard navigation, screen reader support, and semantic HTML.

**Approach:**
- shadcn/ui (Radix primitives) provides accessible components out of the box
- Semantic HTML (form, fieldset, legend, label, button)
- ARIA attributes where needed (step indicator, live regions for price updates)
- Keyboard navigation through all steps
- Focus management on step transitions
- Color contrast (shadcn defaults meet AA)

---

## 18. Project Structure

**Decision:** Standard Laravel Breeze + Inertia structure

**Status:** DECIDED

**Recommendation:**
```
/
  app/
    Http/
      Controllers/
      Requests/          (Form Request validation)
    Models/
    Services/
      ChannelManagerService.php
    Jobs/
  resources/
    js/
      Components/
        Booking/
          DateStep.tsx
          RoomStep.tsx
          SummaryStep.tsx
          StatusStep.tsx
          BookingWizard.tsx
        ui/              (shadcn components)
      Pages/
        Booking/
          Index.tsx
      Hooks/
      Lib/
        schemas/         (Zod schemas)
        utils/
      Stores/            (Zustand)
    css/
  routes/
    web.php              (Inertia page routes)
    api.php              (Booking status polling)
  database/
    migrations/
  tests/
    Feature/             (Pest)
    Unit/                (Pest)
  resources/js/__tests__/ (Vitest)
  CLAUDE.md
  key-decisions.md
  open-questions.md
  docs/
    ai-sessions/         (exported transcripts)
```

---

## Decisions Summary

| # | Decision | Status | Choice |
|---|----------|--------|--------|
| 1 | Frontend framework | DECIDED | React + TypeScript |
| 2 | Architecture | PENDING | Leaning Breeze + Inertia hybrid |
| 3 | Authentication | DECIDED | Laravel Breeze |
| 4 | Database | DECIDED | SQLite |
| 5 | Component library | DECIDED | shadcn/ui + Tailwind |
| 6 | UI design | PENDING | Leaning shadcn + orange branding |
| 7 | Form handling | DECIDED | React Hook Form + Zod |
| 8 | State management | DECIDED | Zustand + persist |
| 9 | State persistence | DECIDED | localStorage via Zustand |
| 10 | Background jobs | DECIDED | Queue + polling |
| 11 | Caching | DECIDED | Laravel file cache + headers |
| 12 | Price calculation | DECIDED | Server-authoritative |
| 13 | Testing | DECIDED | Pest (backend) + Vitest (frontend) |
| 14 | Error handling | DECIDED | Structured responses, no Sentry |
| 15 | Loading states | DECIDED | Spinners |
| 16 | AI showcase | PENDING | Awaiting scope decision |
| 17 | Accessibility | DECIDED | WCAG 2.1 AA |
| 18 | Project structure | DECIDED | Standard Breeze + Inertia |

---

## Future Enhancements (Document for README)

Things to mention as "what I'd add with more time":
- Redis caching in production (replace file driver)
- Sentry error monitoring
- WebSocket-based real-time booking status (Laravel Echo)
- Rate limiting on booking endpoints
- E2E test suite with Playwright
- i18n (Afrikaans/English) - relevant to LekkeSlaap's bilingual platform
- Payment integration
- Booking cancellation flow
- Email notifications (booking confirmation, status updates)
- Mobile-responsive optimization
- Performance monitoring (Web Vitals)
- Loading skeletons for content-heavy pages
- Server-side session backup for cross-device state persistence
